picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL delivered a 93.6% Q1 EPS beat at $5.11/share, Google Cloud surged 63% to $20B revenue with a $462B backlog nearly doubling sequentially, and operating margin expanded to 32.9%. Berkshire tripled its stake to ~$16.6B and Third Point initiated a new position in Q1 2026 — making this the single most crowded institutional buy of the quarter. The stock is up ~30% over the past six months, still generating genuine AI monetization, and trades at a more reasonable multiple than peers given the PEG improvement from cloud margin expansion.
picks.catalyst: Google Cloud backlog conversion into revenue and continued AI Overviews/Search monetization in Q2 2026 earnings (late July)
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~27-28x forward EPS — rich vs. 10-year average but justified by cloud margin tripling to 32.9% from 17.8% YoY; consolidating above the 50dma after +30% 6-month run
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or antitrust breakup remedies materially impair Search monetization
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust remedies forcing structural separation of Search and/or Android distribution

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom posted a 9.04% Q1 EPS beat at $2.05 vs $1.88 estimated, with a PEG ratio of just 0.77 despite +106% TTM price return — signaling growth is still underpriced relative to trajectory. ROIC of 21.33% and ROE of 33.37% confirm capital efficiency. Druckenmiller opened a new position in AVGO in Q1 2026, rotating from Nvidia into Broadcom as a more diversified AI infrastructure play across custom silicon (XPUs for Google/Meta/ByteDance) and VMware cloud integration. AI networking spend is a separate, additive driver with 800G optical opportunity seen nearly doubling in 2026.
picks.catalyst: June 3 fiscal Q2 2026 earnings report — custom AI ASIC revenue update and VMware integration synergies
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~31.9x forward P/E vs. historical range of 18-35x; near-term consolidation after +106% TTM run creates a reasonable entry ahead of a high-visibility earnings catalyst
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of custom XPU design wins at Google or Meta, or VMware customer churn above 15%
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — top 3 hyperscaler XPU customers represent majority of AI revenue; any single cancellation is a material earnings event

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA just reported Q1 FY27 revenue of $81.6B (+85% YoY) with $49B free cash flow and 74.9% gross margins, and guided Q2 to $91B vs. $86.84B consensus — a clean upside beat with forward momentum intact. Wedbush called NVDA 'the leading supplier of AI silicon' with a trillion-dollar order book through 2027. The AI infrastructure supercycle nowcast of +11.4% Q2 real gross private domestic investment corroborates sustained hyperscaler capex of $725B budgeted for 2026, up 77%. Position is sized at medium-high rather than maximum given the 'buy the rumor, sell the news' post-earnings dynamic risk and 66% one-year run.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY27 guidance of $91B converts to reported revenue — next print in August 2026, plus Blackwell Ultra ramp confirmation
picks.entryNote: Trading around $135-145 range post-May 20 print; stock has historically seen post-earnings chop before resuming trend — current entry is into the post-report consolidation window
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin compression below 70% or Q3 guidance below $90B, signaling Blackwell supply constraints exceeding demand
picks.keyRisk: Post-earnings 'sell the news' sentiment reset; hyperscaler debt-financed capex could be trimmed if rates stay elevated and FCF pressure mounts

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a high-conviction defense compounder with continuous contract flow — a $441.6M PATRIOT GEM-T modification for Operation Epic Fury, a $335M Standard Missile modification, SeaRAM frigate contracts with Australia, and Space Force interceptor program selection all announced in May 2026. The DoD's aggressive shift toward autonomous AI-driven tactical networks and hypersonic programs directly benefits RTX's Raytheon segment. The 12-month analyst consensus target of $215.27 implies 22.3% upside from current levels, with a 23-analyst Buy consensus and a 7.4% dividend increase just declared. Middle East conflict spending mandates are a structural, multi-year tailwind.
picks.catalyst: CEO Chris Calio speaking at Bernstein Strategic Decisions Conference May 29 — expected to provide backlog and margin guidance update
picks.catalystWindow: May 29, 2026 (next 7 days) then Q2 earnings July
picks.entryNote: Trading roughly 15-17% below consensus analyst price target; forward P/E ~20x on defense earnings that are recession-resistant; consolidating above long-term support after steady re-rating higher
picks.exitTrigger: Middle East ceasefire reducing defense procurement urgency, or GTF engine liability charges materially exceeding $7B reserve
picks.keyRisk: Pratt & Whitney GTF powder metal engine liability remains an open-ended balance sheet risk that could resurface with incremental charges

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Financials are the #2 ranked sector in the rotation screen, with Goldman specifically positioned as the premier capital markets beneficiary of deal flow reactivation and a steeper yield curve. Outside of IT and Energy, Financials is the only sector with positive dollar-level earnings revisions since December 31. Goldman's trading and advisory desks benefit directly from volatility and geopolitical uncertainty — regimes that are suppressive for most sectors are additive to Goldman's revenue mix. The sector trades at a discount to the broader market with improving EPS and dividend trajectory, and institutional flows are reaccelerating with SCHD's 2026 reconstitution raising Financials from 9.7% to 15%+.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings report (mid-July) — trading revenue and M&A advisory pipeline against backdrop of reactivating deal markets
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~10-11x forward earnings — meaningful discount to SPX at ~20x; yield curve steepening (2s10s turning positive) directly expands NII for banking conglomerates; technically in an uptrend
picks.exitTrigger: Credit cycle turns with HY OAS widening above 450bps, or capital markets deal flow stalls in a recessionary environment
picks.keyRisk: Late-cycle credit deterioration — Goldman's trading book has tail risk to a rapid HY spread blow-out if recession materializes faster than consensus expects

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY is trading at ~$1,044 today (May 22) after its next-generation retatrutide weight-loss drug cleared a late-stage Phase 3 clinical trial — a direct pipeline catalyst adding to the already-dominant GLP-1 oligopoly with Mounjaro and Zepbound. The company has committed ~$23B in manufacturing capex to expand fill-finish capacity through 2027, removing the supply ceiling that capped 2025 revenue. Unlike NVO, which faces semaglutide patent cliffs and MFN pricing pressure, LLY benefits from NVO's headwinds by capturing market share. Druckenmiller increased his LLY stake to 100,675 shares in Q1 2026, validating the quality compounder thesis.
picks.catalyst: Retatrutide Phase 3 full data readout and potential FDA filing timeline — plus Q2 earnings in late July with Zepbound volume trajectory
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$1,044, well below 52-week high of $1,133; ~30-35x forward earnings is reasonable for a 20%+ earnings growth compounder with secular obesity + Alzheimer's pipeline; bouncing off multi-month support
picks.exitTrigger: MFN pricing executive order expands to commercial markets, or oral GLP-1 competitor (e.g., Pfizer danuglipron) achieves superior efficacy data in Phase 3
picks.keyRisk: Medicare/Medicaid GLP-1 coverage rollback under 'One Big Beautiful Bill' reduces total addressable market and realized pricing

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a capital-light, high-ROIC compounder with structural revenue tied to debt issuance, index licensing, and data analytics — all tailwinds in a late-cycle environment where corporate debt issuance remains robust and passive index AUM continues compounding. HY OAS at 276bps (near multi-year tights) and IG OAS at ~80bps reflect a functioning credit market still generating ratings and issuance fees. SPGI's Market Intelligence and Commodity Insights segments diversify revenue beyond pure credit cycles. The business has pricing power embedded in long-term contracts and a near-monopoly in index licensing alongside MSCI.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — debt issuance volumes in Q2 driven by corporate refinancing activity and sovereign bond supply will directly confirm revenue trajectory
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~28-30x forward earnings — below its 5-year average premium of 33x; credit market stability at current spread levels supports a stable issuance fee baseline going into Q2
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS widens sharply above 400bps (credit stress kills issuance), or regulatory challenge to ratings oligopoly business model
picks.keyRisk: A sharp credit cycle turn would reduce debt issuance and ratings fees simultaneously — SPGI's earnings are more cyclical than its quality multiple implies

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is a portfolio anchor against the Middle East conflict / energy price tail risk — gas prices are up 28.4% YoY averaging $4.50/gallon, and Energy sector Q2 estimate revisions have been positive since the start of the Iran war. XOM trades at ~11x forward P/E with a 3.4% dividend yield and best-in-class integration across upstream, refining, and chemicals, making it the most resilient major if oil prices spike on conflict escalation. The Pioneer acquisition has added 1.3B BOE of Permian inventory at low breakevens (~$35/bbl), providing durable FCF even in a $70 crude scenario.
picks.catalyst: April Core PCE print (May 30) — a hot print above 3.1% removes rate cut expectations entirely, supporting energy sector multiple; any Middle East escalation is an immediate price catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: May 30 PCE print and rolling geopolitical news flow
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~11x forward P/E vs. sector median of 12-13x and 5-year XOM average of 14x; Chevron has been trimmed by both Berkshire and institutional investors — XOM is the higher-quality alternative at a similar valuation
picks.exitTrigger: WTI crude sustains below $65/bbl for 60+ days, implying demand destruction that compresses XOM FCF below $30B annualized
picks.keyRisk: Global growth deceleration (particularly China demand) brings crude below $65 — XOM's Permian breakeven is low but the multiple would still compress

picks.name: Vistra Energy Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is the purest public market play on AI data center power demand growth — owning a mix of nuclear, gas, and retail electricity that directly benefits from the +11.4% Q2 real gross private domestic investment nowcast driven by AI capex. Tepper doubled his VST position in Q1 2026, and the DoE has flagged US power grid capacity as the binding constraint on AI infrastructure scaling. Unlike regulated utilities, VST has merchant price upside when power demand surges. Data center power purchase agreements (PPAs) are being signed at record premiums as hyperscalers compete for firm capacity.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (August) — power price realizations and new data center PPA announcements; any federal grid expansion legislation accelerates the thesis
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~19x forward earnings — above historical utility comps but below tech multiples despite a fundamentally tech-linked revenue driver; technically consolidating after a strong 2025 re-rating
picks.exitTrigger: Power price normalization as new generation capacity comes online faster than demand grows, or nuclear plant regulatory/safety incident at Comanche Peak
picks.keyRisk: Merchant power price volatility — unlike regulated utilities, VST has no guaranteed rate of return; a warm winter or demand miss could compress realized prices meaningfully

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon's AWS cloud growth is on a clean acceleration trajectory — 20% in Q3 2025, 24% in Q4 2025, and +28% in Q1 2026 — with Trainium revenue commitments topping $225B, giving AMZN a proprietary AI chip advantage complementary to Nvidia. Ackman added ~1.84M shares to move AMZN to his #2 position in Q1 2026 while exiting Hilton, explicitly rotating into cloud/AI compounders with durable pricing power. The stock is up ~15% over the past six months, outperforming SPY, with AWS operating margin expansion continuing to drive overall EBIT improvement. The DXY at ~99.31 (mild dollar softness) is a tailwind for AMZN's international advertising and retail segments.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July/early August) — AWS growth trajectory above 30% YoY would be a significant positive re-rating event
picks.entryNote: Trading ~15% above its 6-month-ago level but below pre-tariff-scare highs; AWS at 28% growth warrants a premium to historical multiple; retail margin compression is the known headwind already in consensus
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 22% YoY for two consecutive quarters, signaling Azure/GCP market share gains or enterprise cloud spending pullback
picks.keyRisk: Retail segment margin compression from tariff-driven COGS increases and consumer spending deceleration (Michigan Sentiment at 53.3) limiting total FCF growth

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the portfolio's primary macro hedge against the three concurrent tail risks: fiscal dominance (US debt ceiling/downgrade risk), Middle East conflict escalation, and stagflation scenario where both equities and bonds underperform. The Iran conflict provides a persistent geopolitical bid while central bank buying provides a structural floor. GLD's recent ~8% 30-day pullback on dollar strength creates a better entry than the Q1 highs, and the 30-year Treasury above 4.88% with rising term premium is the exact environment where gold's store-of-value function is most valuable.
picks.catalyst: April Core PCE (May 30) — a hot print above 3.1% removes all 2026 rate cut probability, repricing gold higher as a stagflation hedge; any Middle East escalation is an immediate catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: May 30 PCE print; rolling geopolitical news
picks.entryNote: GLD near ~$310-315 range — pulled back ~8% over 30 days from highs; technically testing support near the 50dma, which historically has been a favorable entry in uptrend regimes
picks.exitTrigger: Durable Middle East ceasefire combined with core PCE declining to 2.3% or below — both conditions simultaneously removing the stagflation and geopolitical premium
picks.keyRisk: Sharp USD strength on a risk-off flight-to-safety event (paradoxically) compresses gold in dollar terms short-term before the inflation thesis reasserts

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD is up 8.50% YTD, consistently outperforming the broader market in 2026, with its 2026 reconstitution rotating capital toward Healthcare, Tech, and Financials while trimming Energy by ~8% — a deliberate quality upgrade. US dividend income funds pulled in $24.1B in Q1 2026, the biggest Q1 inflow in four years, providing flow tailwind. In a late-cycle regime with frozen Fed policy, SCHD's 3-4% yield and quality factor tilt (TXN, QCOM, KO, CVX) provides income with meaningful drawdown protection against a concentrated Tech/AI correction in the growth book.
picks.catalyst: Risk-off rotation events (hot PCE print, geopolitical escalation) drive dividend ETF inflows; SCHD's reconstituted portfolio is well-positioned to absorb capital flight from speculative growth
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; heightened during May 30 PCE and June 17-18 FOMC
picks.entryNote: Trading at a modest premium to NAV with +8.5% YTD already; buying the quality/income factor at current levels makes sense given the late-cycle positioning — not cheap in absolute terms, but cheap relative to duration risk in long bonds
picks.exitTrigger: Fed begins aggressive easing (50bps+ cuts), triggering a growth/momentum rotation that structurally underweights quality/dividend factors for 12+ months
picks.keyRisk: Energy sector exposure (~12-13% post-reconstitution) provides residual commodity correlation if oil prices fall sharply

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire's $373.3B cash position is the loudest macro signal in the market — Greg Abel's first full quarter as CEO saw $16B in buys and $24B in sells, simplifying the portfolio to 26 positions. The record cash pile earns 5%+ in T-bills while preserving extreme optionality to deploy in a dislocation. BRK.B's operating earnings from BNSF, GEICO, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, and consumer businesses are structurally recession-resistant, while the GOOGL position ($16.6B) gives equity upside. Trading at ~1.5x book — well below historical premium — with buybacks resumed in March 2026, this is the closest thing to a risk-managed equity exposure in the universe.
picks.catalyst: Annual meeting follow-up capital deployment signals; any market dislocation where Berkshire's cash becomes an acquisition weapon is a catalyst for multiple re-rating
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; any market drawdown of 10%+ is a Berkshire catalyst
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~1.5x book value — historically BRK.B has traded 1.3-2.0x book; current entry is in the lower half of the historical range, providing margin of safety
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel demonstrates materially worse capital allocation than Buffett's historical record (e.g., large acquisition at full valuation); or insurance catastrophe losses exceeding $30B in a single year
picks.keyRisk: CEO transition risk under Greg Abel — first full year of independent decision-making; any large mispriced acquisition would be a sentiment negative

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides the portfolio's only explicit long-duration rate exposure and functions as an equity-market crash hedge — the one scenario where both AI-growth names and energy stocks sell off simultaneously (a severe recession) would drive a flight to Treasuries. With the 10-year yield at ~4.20%, IEF offers a ~4.1% running yield while providing negative correlation to risk assets in tail scenarios. The position is sized at medium-weight given the macro regime — term premium is rising and fiscal dominance risk is real — but it earns carry while the hedge premium is inexpensive at current spread levels. June FOMC (June 17-18) under new Chair Warsh is the first key signal on whether the Fed pivots hawkish or signals any easing tolerance.
picks.catalyst: June 17-18 FOMC — any dovish pivot by Chair Warsh or downgrade to the inflation outlook would compress the 10-year yield and generate capital gains on IEF
picks.entryNote: 10-year at ~4.20% — near the upper end of the 2026 range; buying IEF at 4.20% yield provides attractive carry relative to history; sized small given the fiscal supply headwind
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yield breaks above 5% on a sustained basis, signaling fiscal dominance regime where bonds and equities fall together — exit IEF, add GLD
picks.keyRisk: Fiscal dominance scenario — US sovereign downgrade pressure and rising term premium push the 10-year to 5%+, making IEF a losing position even in a risk-off environment

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the portfolio's pure consumer staples anchor — 63+ consecutive years of dividend growth, global distribution moat, and confirmed pricing power in a 2.8% CPI / 3.0% core PCE environment. With Michigan Consumer Sentiment at 53.3 and food prices up 3.2% YoY, KO's non-discretionary beverage demand is relatively insulated from consumer spending weakness. It features as one of SCHD's largest positions precisely because of its role as a 'sleep at night' low-beta compounder. The ~3.1% dividend yield provides income and the stock tends to rotate up in risk-off environments when discretionary spending concerns dominate headlines.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — volume growth confirmation in international markets and pricing vs. volume trade-off update amid inflation environment
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~24x forward earnings — above historical average of ~22x but premium is justified by quality of cash flows and defensive factor demand; technically stable with consistent accumulation
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained volume declines of 5%+ globally for two consecutive quarters as private-label competition or GLP-1 drug adoption materially impairs beverage consumption
picks.keyRisk: Private-label substitution pressure and GLP-1 drug adoption reducing sugar/calorie consumption — a structural long-term volume headwind that could compress the revenue growth multiple

summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle / pre-contraction regime with a geopolitical overlay — sticky inflation at 2.8% core CPI, a frozen Fed under new Chair Warsh, and Middle East conflict energy costs all argue for quality cash flows over duration and speculative growth. The 10-name growth book concentrates in the three highest-conviction secular themes: AI infrastructure (GOOGL, AVGO, NVDA, AMZN — differentiated across search/cloud, custom silicon, GPU, and hyperscale), defense/geopolitical spending (RTX), financials cycle (GS, SPGI), healthcare compounders (LLY), energy hedge (XOM), and AI power demand (VST). All seven core carries from the prior week are maintained — GOOGL, AVGO, RTX, GS, LLY, SPGI, XOM/VST — with NVDA upgraded to high-weight (rank 3) post its clean May 20 earnings beat and AMZN re-entered at rank 10 following Ackman's Q1 accumulation and AWS acceleration to +28%. The dominant risk being underwritten is a fiscal dominance / US sovereign re-rating event where both long bonds and equities re-price simultaneously — mitigated by GLD as the primary hedge, IEF sized small for crash-scenario carry, SCHD for quality income, BRK.B as a cash-option compounder, and KO as the consumer staples anchor.
diversificationNote: Growth book spans 6 distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL), Information Technology (AVGO, NVDA — differentiated across custom ASIC and GPU silicon), Industrials (RTX), Financials (GS, SPGI), Health Care (LLY), Energy (XOM), Utilities (VST), and Consumer Discretionary (AMZN); the two IT names share secular AI exposure but operate at different points in the value chain (networking/custom silicon vs. training/inference GPU). Defensive sleeve spans four exposures: gold (GLD), broad dividend/quality income (SCHD), diversified insurance/equity compounder (BRK.B), intermediate treasuries (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — no exposure is doubled up.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing as of Friday, May 22, 2026.

---

## 📊 US EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING — May 22, 2026

---

### DATA DASHBOARD

**FED POLICY**

The FOMC held the target range for the fed funds rate at **3.50%–3.75%** at its March 18, 2026 meeting.
 
The March 2026 dot plot median signals only **25 bps of cuts in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027**, with the Fed projecting PCE inflation to end 2026 at **2.7%** — 30 bps higher than its December 2025 projection.
 However, 
markets now broadly expect no cuts this year, with one analyst noting: "The trajectory of inflation will not immediately reverse, even if geopolitical tensions ease, making it highly unlikely that we will see any interest rate cuts this year."
 The Fed's longer-run neutral rate is estimated around 3.0%, implying the real policy rate (~+0.8% above 2.7% projected inflation) remains only modestly restrictive. 
Importantly, Kevin Warsh assumed the Fed Chair role on May 15, replacing Jerome Powell.


**YIELD CURVE**

As of May 21, the US yield curve shape is *Normal* — both 2s10s and 3m10y are nominally positive — with the current policy rate at **4.25%** (upper bound).
 
The 10-year yield sits roughly around **4.20%**, while the 2s10s spread is positive but narrow, and the 3m10y has only just crossed below zero.
 
That combination — front-end inverted, belly flat, long end slightly higher — is a *classic late-cycle signature*. The 3m10y spread has flipped negative again in recent weeks after a brief positive excursion in early 2026.
 The 30-year was at **~4.88%** as of mid-March. Term premium is directionally rising — driven by fiscal supply, energy-shock uncertainty, and the Fed leadership transition. 
The 2s10s, which inverted continuously between mid-2022 and late-2024, is now narrowly positive but well below the 100–150 bp range typical of a healthy expansion.


**INFLATION**

April CPI: Core CPI rose **+0.4% m/m**, with inflation ticking up 0.2 ppt to **2.8% y/y**.
 
Shelter costs rose 0.6% m/m, though a temporary statistical distortion tied to last fall's government shutdown lifted shelter CPI in April — a one-off effect unlikely to signal underlying momentum.
 
The bifurcation between goods and services is the new feature of April 2026: goods are flat or declining while services accelerate — consistent with a tariff regime absorbed in margins, layered onto a services-side cost shock being passed through directly.
 
Core PCE stood at **3.0% YoY** through February
; 
April core PCE (due May 30) will likely run cooler than +0.4% core CPI, with consensus expecting roughly **0.25–0.35% m/m**, pushing YoY toward **2.9–3.1%**.
 
Supercore (services ex-shelter) showed no meaningful deterioration in March
, but 
services prices in April revealed early passthrough from the Middle East conflict, with airfare rising strongly.
 Inflation is **re-accelerating** at the headline level (energy/services shock), sticky at core, with a tail risk of entrenchment.

**GROWTH**

The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimate for real GDP growth (SAAR) in Q2 2026 is **+4.3%** as of May 21, up from 4.0% on May 14.
 
Nowcasts of Q2 real PCE growth and real gross private domestic investment growth increased to **2.9%** and **11.4%**, respectively.
 
The BEA's first estimate of Q1 2026 real GDP growth was **+2.0%**, 0.8 ppt above the final GDPNow nowcast.
 The payrolls run-rate remains solid (~175–185K/month average over recent months), with unemployment projected by the Fed at 4.4% in Q4 2026. ISM Manufacturing remains in contraction territory near 48–49; ISM Services is modestly expansionary near 51–52.

**CREDIT & LIQUIDITY**

ICE BofA US HY OAS was **~2.76%** (276 bps) in May 2026
 — near multi-year tights and not signaling stress. 
Broad investment-grade OAS is roughly **~80 bps** as of early 2026, with tighter spreads for AA issuers and wider for BBB.
 Financial conditions remain broadly accommodative. 
The DXY (USD index) closed at approximately **99.31** on May 22
, reflecting mild dollar softness — a tailwind for US multinationals and EM.

**EARNINGS**

The S&P 500 forward four-quarter EPS estimate (FFQE) closed last week at approximately **$346.82**, with the earnings yield at **4.68%** as of May 15.
 
Excluding non-operating gains from GOOG, AMZN, and NFLX, Q1 y-o-y S&P 500 EPS growth falls to approximately **10%**, with 2026 annual growth likely around 22%.
 
The trailing S&P 500 earnings yield is **3.71%** as of May 22
, implying a trailing P/E near **~27x** — stretched against a 10-year average closer to 18–19x forward. 
As of March 31, equities were assessed as *Fairly Valued* on an earnings yield gap basis, 0.30 standard deviations below historical average.


---

### ¶1 — REGIME CALL

**This is a late-cycle regime with a geopolitical overlay.** 
The yield curve exhibits a classic late-cycle signature: front-end effectively inverted on 3m10y, the 2s10s narrowly positive after continuous inversion from 2022–2024, and the 3m10y flipping back negative in recent weeks.
 The policy rate at 3.50–3.75% remains above neutral (~3.0%), labor markets are still tight but moderating (Fed projects 4.4% unemployment by year-end), 
goods prices are flat or declining while services are accelerating
 — a textbook late-expansion price dynamic. 
The dot plot pencils in only 50 bps of total cuts through 2027, with PCE stuck at 2.7% this year
, and 
market pricing has effectively removed rate cuts from the 2026 calendar entirely.
 The combination of positive but decelerating real growth, sticky-to-rising inflation, a frozen Fed, and a leadership transition at the FOMC is a *late-cycle / pre-contraction* positioning environment — not yet a contraction, but with material fat tails.

---

### ¶2 — WHAT'S WORKING / WHAT'S BREAKING

In a late-cycle, above-neutral-rate, sticky-inflation regime, the playbook favors **quality, cash flow durability, and real asset exposure over duration and pure growth**. What's **working**: (1) **Large-cap Energy and Defense** — 
gas prices are up ~28.4% YoY and averaging $4.50/gallon
, directly supporting Energy sector earnings and defense spending mandates linked to the Middle East conflict; (2) **AI Infrastructure / Hyperscalers** — 
Q2 real gross private domestic investment is nowcasting at +11.4%
, with AI capex the primary driver, and 
EPS growth ex-non-operating items is still ~10% y/y
; (3) **Short-duration IG Credit** — 
IG OAS at ~80 bps
 is tight but credit is not breaking, and carry is attractive at current all-in yields; (4) **Consumer Staples / Pricing Power** as a defensive hedge. What's **breaking**: (1) **Rate-sensitive / long-duration equities** (REITs, utilities, speculative growth) — 
a 10-year yield above 5% would be meaningfully negative, especially amid Fed leadership transition and curve steepening
; (2) **Consumer Discretionary** — 
food prices up 3.2% YoY and beef up 14.8%
 are squeezing real purchasing power; (3) **HY issuers with floating-rate debt** — spreads are tight, but refinancing at 3.50%+ policy rates is compressing margins in leveraged capital structures.

---

### ¶3 — WHAT TO WATCH (2–3 Highest-Impact Events, Next 1–6 Months)

1. **April Core PCE (May 30)** — 
The Fed's preferred gauge; PCE weights shelter less heavily than CPI, so April core PCE will likely run cooler than +0.4% core CPI, but consensus expects YoY ticking toward **2.9–3.1%** from March's 3.0%.
 A print above 3.1% would effectively end any remaining H2 2026 cut probability and reprice the long end materially higher.

2. **Iran/Middle East Conflict Resolution — Energy Price Trajectory** — 
Economists note inflationary effects of the war could take weeks to months to unwind, with an optimistic normalization timeline of ~2 months if resolved soon, or six to nine months in a pessimistic scenario.
 
Lingering geopolitical tensions in the Middle East are likely to add further upward pressure through higher fuel costs, feeding into transportation prices in the coming months.
 An escalation or a durable ceasefire is the single highest-variance input to the inflation trajectory and Fed optionality.

3. **New Fed Chair Warsh's First Communications / FOMC Policy Signals** — 
Warsh assumed the Chair role on May 15; his first communications will be parsed carefully for whether he frames April's inflation data as an energy outlier (dovish read) or a services breakout (hawkish read).
 Any pivot in the Fed's reaction function under new leadership — particularly on the inflation tolerance threshold — is the key institutional risk for the rate/equity complex over the next 1–6 months. Watch the June FOMC (June 17–18) and the updated SEP dot plot as the definitive signal.

---
*All data as of market close May 22, 2026. Forward-looking statements are analytical, not investment advice.*

## 🥇 TIER 1 — OVERWEIGHT / STRONG BUY

---

### **#1 — Technology / AI Infrastructure / Semis (XLK, SOXX)**
**RS Tier: LEADER (recovering to leader)**


FactSet expects Information Technology to deliver the strongest Q1 2026 earnings growth in the S&P 500 at around 45%.
 
Most of the increase in earnings expectations has been concentrated in Information Technology, which has recorded the second-largest (+8.0%) increase in expected dollar-level earnings of all eleven sectors since December 31 — and has the highest number of companies issuing positive EPS guidance for the quarter at 33.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
Based on FactSet consensus for 2026, the technology sector has the lowest P/E-to-growth ratio of all sectors. The P/E on 2026 estimates is near 27, but earnings are expected to grow by a similar +28.3% — making valuations reasonable, in the upper half of the 10-year average but not stretched.
 
In Q2 2026, over $12 billion flowed into technology ETFs
 — the largest institutional commitment of any sector.
- **Why Now:** 
Information Technology supply chain pressure in the second half of 2026 could introduce new challenges,
 but with AI capex still accelerating, semis rebounding post-tariff scare, and the sector now recovering price leadership after a rough Q1, this is the highest-conviction re-entry point for a 1–5 year horizon.

---

### **#2 — Financials (Banks / Capital Markets / Insurance / Fintech) (XLF)**
**RS Tier: COILING → LEADER**


Modest increases in interest rates and a steeper yield curve are driving net interest income for banks and insurance company returns on policyholder premium balances. Structural trends in digitalization and financial technology are creating new revenue opportunities, resulting in improved EPS and dividend payouts. The sector has lower valuations relative to the broader market.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
Outside of IT and Energy, Financials is the only other sector that has recorded an increase in dollar-level earnings since December 31 (+0.4%).
 
Within SCHD's 2026 reconstitution, Financials are projected to climb from 9.7% to 15%+ — a signal of institutional reallocation toward the sector.

- **Why Now:** Capital markets are firing on deal activity, insurance is benefiting from the rate environment, and the sector trades at a discount to the market — the trifecta for a mid-to-late cycle financial sector that hasn't yet fully re-rated.

---

### **#3 — Industrials — Defense / Electrification sub-focus (XLI, ITA)**
**RS Tier: LEADER (extended, watch for consolidation)**


Industrial demand is broad-based, driven by AI-fueled data center buildouts and higher defense spending. The growth outlook is structurally stronger than in the previous decade, supported by megatrends such as decarbonization, electrification, digitalization, and re-industrialization.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
Strong investor interest has recently driven rapid fund inflows and pushed valuations to near-record levels.
 
XLI had a total return of 29.87% in the past year including dividends.

- **Why Now:** 
Industrials may underperform if tariffs eventually eat into profit margins and the manufacturing sector's recovery takes longer than expected,
 but the defense/electrification sub-theme is a multi-year secular story that transcends the cycle — trim the pure cyclical machinery names, stay long defense and power infrastructure.

---

## 🥈 TIER 2 — OVERWEIGHT / ACCUMULATE

---

### **#4 — Communication Services (XLC)**
**RS Tier: LEADER**


All eleven sectors are projected to report year-over-year revenue growth, led by Information Technology, Communication Services, and Financials.
 
Strategic rebalancing continues to favor Communication Services, which remains rated Outperform by Schwab, reflecting solid fundamentals and exposure to AI adoption.


- **Valuation/Flow:** 
The sector is highly concentrated, with over 70% of its weight attributed to just two stocks, raising idiosyncratic risk,
 yet advertising revenue tailwinds and streaming profitability make this a quality growth play.
- **Why Now:** 
Communication Services has high exposure to advertising, which can be pressured during slowdowns, and faces competitive pressures in mature markets like telecom and media
 — but Meta and Alphabet's AI integration is generating genuine monetization, making this the cheapest way to buy AI application-layer earnings.

---

**RS Tier: LAGGING → COILING (deep value setup)**


Year-to-date in 2026, XLV is down about 7% while SPY is up about 5%
 — one of the widest single-year divergences on record. But that gap is precisely the opportunity.

- **Valuation/Flow:** 
Despite improving economics, healthcare still trades at a discount to technology and consumer discretionary on forward earnings multiples, while offering stronger earnings stability and lower volatility — creating a compelling entry point of discounted valuation and rising profitability.
 
82% of public biotech companies are now estimated to have "very good" drug pipelines, compared to only 47% in 2022.

for 2025, M&A activity in biotech already surpassed 2024 and could maintain its pace into 2026, as large-cap pharma faces pressure to replace hundreds of billions in revenues losing patent protection.

the "One Big Beautiful Bill" will cut Medicaid spending by an estimated $1.02 trillion,
 a direct earnings headwind for managed care operators.

XLV's Aroon Indicator entered a downward trend on May 8, 2026
 — technical bottom-fishing risk is real near-term, but for a 1–5 year horizon, demographic tailwinds and biotech M&A make this the single-best valuation opportunity in the market.

Real assets are leading the charge in 2026, with gold, metals, and mining companies posting "outstanding" performances

**RS Tier: COILING**


Domestic-focused power infrastructure and solar players can benefit from structural growth drivers like AI data center expansion, industrial expansion, and the energy transition.
 
Defensive sectors like utilities are underperforming as investors seek higher growth elsewhere. Rising bond yields have further reduced the appeal of utility stocks.

 The 30-year was at **~4.88%** as of mid-March. Term premium is directionally rising — driven by fiscal supply, energy-shock uncertainty, and the Fed leadership transition.

, but 
services prices in April revealed early passthrough from the Middle East conflict, with airfare rising strongly.

goods prices are flat or declining while services are accelerating
 — a textbook late-expansion price dynamic. 
The dot plot pencils in only 50 bps of total cuts through 2027, with PCE stuck at 2.7% this year
, and 
market pricing has effectively removed rate cuts from the 2026 calendar entirely.
 The combination of positive but decelerating real growth, sticky-to-rising inflation, a frozen Fed, and a leadership transition at the FOMC is a *late-cycle / pre-contraction* positioning environment — not yet a contraction, but with material fat tails.

In a late-cycle, above-neutral-rate, sticky-inflation regime, the playbook favors **quality, cash flow durability, and real asset exposure over duration and pure growth**. What's **working**: (1) **Large-cap Energy and Defense** —
